perturbation.original_text: For a period of four years from the date of this Agreement (the "Restricted Period"), Seller Parties shall not, directly or indirectly, engage in The Company's Business (as hereinafter defined) or, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, directly or indirectly, own an interest in, manage, operate, join, control, lend money or render financial or other assistance to or participate in, as a partner, stockholder, consultant or otherwise, any Person that competes with The Company's Business as it is conducted as of the Closing Date.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of four years from the date of this Agreement (the "Restricted Period"), Seller Parties shall not, directly or indirectly, engage in The Company's Business (as hereinafter defined) or, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, directly or indirectly, own an interest in, manage, operate, join, control, lend money or render financial or other assistance to or participate in, as a partner, stockholder, consultant or otherwise, any Person that competes with The Company's Business as it is conducted as of the Closing Date, unless the Seller Parties provide a royalty fee of 0.5% of gross profits from the competing business to the Buyer Parties.
perturbation.explanation: By adding a condition where the Seller Parties can compete by paying a royalty fee, we create ambiguity and potentially contradict the fundamental purpose of a non-compete agreement. This undermines its enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sherman Antitrust Act prohibits contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade. While non-compete agreements are generally permissible within reasonable bounds, the modification introduces a royalty fee as a way to circumvent the non-compete. A court could interpret this as an unreasonable restraint of trade if the royalty is deemed excessive or anticompetitive, making the non-compete clause questionable under antitrust law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a royalty fee as a means to circumvent the original non-compete agreement, potentially conflicting with the Sherman Antitrust Act. The original text strictly prohibits competition, while the changed text allows it under a payment structure. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal.' By allowing competition for a fee, the altered contract may be viewed as a conditional restraint of trade, violating the Act if the royalty fee is deemed excessive or anticompetitive. Therefore, the modification weakens the non-compete clause and potentially restrains trade in a manner that could be considered illegal under the Sherman Antitrust Act, creating a legal contradiction as it undermines the initial non-compete agreement's intent.

perturbation.original_text: During the Restricted Period and at all times thereafter, Seller Parties will keep and hold all Confidential Information (as hereinafter defined) in strict confidence, and will not use or disclose in any way any of such Confidential Information without the prior express written consent of the Buyer Parties. For the purposes of this Agreement, the term "Confidential Information" shall mean all trade secrets and confidential or proprietary information (and any tangible representation thereof) owned, possessed or used in connection with The Company Business or by the Buyer Parties and its Affiliates; provided, however, that "Confidential Information" does not include information which is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a disclosure by a Seller Party.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Restricted Period and at all times thereafter, Seller Parties will keep and hold all Confidential Information in strict confidence, and will not use or disclose in any way any of such Confidential Information without the prior express written consent of the Buyer Parties. For the purposes of this Agreement, the term "Confidential Information" shall mean information the Buyer Parties deem confidential. "Confidential Information" does not include information which is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a disclosure by a Seller Party.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the definition of "Confidential Information" to 'information the Buyer Parties deem confidential' we create an overbroad and potentially unenforceable definition. This contradicts the established legal standards for what constitutes a trade secret or confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) defines a 'trade secret' specifically. The contract modification broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to anything 'the Buyer Parties deem confidential,' which is far broader than the DTSA's definition and therefore unenforceable. The DTSA requires that the information derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The modified definition does not contain those limitations.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines "Confidential Information" in an overbroad manner ('information the Buyer Parties deem confidential'), which contradicts the typical legal understanding of trade secrets. The original definition included trade secrets and confidential/proprietary information related to the Company Business or Buyer Parties, aligning more closely with established legal standards. 18 USC 1839 defines terms related to the protection of trade secrets, implying a specific legal understanding that differs from the changed text's broad definition. The changed definition lacks limitations and criteria for what can be considered confidential, creating a significant departure from legally recognized definitions and potentially rendering the clause unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Seller Parties recognize that money damages alone would not adequately compensate Buyer Parties and its Affiliates in the event of breach by Seller Parties of this Agreement, and Seller Parties therefore agree that, in addition to all other remedies available to Buyer Parties and its Affiliates, at law, in equity or otherwise, Buyer Parties and its Affiliates shall be entitled to injunctive relief for the enforcement hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller Parties recognize that money damages alone would not adequately compensate Buyer Parties and its Affiliates in the event of breach by Seller Parties of this Agreement. Buyer Parties may seek damages only after arbitration. Arbitration will be non-binding.
perturbation.explanation: By specifying non-binding arbitration and suggesting that damage assessment comes only after arbitration, we contradict the principle that injunctive relief is available. This can potentially be viewed as an overreach from the Buyer Parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) favors binding arbitration agreements. By stipulating that arbitration is non-binding and damage assessment can occur after arbitration, this contract attempts to circumvent the enforcement mechanisms typically associated with arbitration under the FAA, creating a conflict in whether the intent is truly to arbitrate.

"Maritime transactions" and "commerce" defined; exceptions to operation of title "Maritime transactions", as herein defined, means charter parties, bills of lading of water carriers, agreements relating to wharfage, supplies furnished vessels or repairs to vessels, collisions, or any other matters in foreign commerce which, if the subject of controversy, would be embraced within admiralty jurisdictio
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly allows for injunctive relief, while the changed text introduces non-binding arbitration as a precursor to seeking damages, thus contradicting the availability of immediate injunctive relief. The FAA snippet defines 'maritime transactions' and 'commerce,' establishing the scope within which arbitration is favored. By making arbitration non-binding, the changed text attempts to circumvent the usual enforcement associated with arbitration, creating a conflict as described in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the substantive laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its principles of conflicts of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of Nevada, notwithstanding that the principal place of business of the relevant parties is in Delaware and Colorado.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Nevada when the parties are in Delaware and Colorado and the contract specifies Delaware creates uncertainty and potential conflict. This can cause issues if Nevada law conflicts with Delaware or Colorado law on key issues in the contract, such as non-compete clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Full Faith and Credit Clause, U.S. Constitution, Article IV, Section 1
perturbation.law_explanation: The Full Faith and Credit Clause requires states to respect the laws of other states. While parties can generally choose a governing law, courts may refuse to enforce that choice if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest in the dispute, such as Delaware where the company is incorporated. Choosing Nevada law seemingly arbitrarily could violate the Full Faith and Credit Clause if it conflicts with Delaware law and Delaware has a significant interest in the case.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article IV | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article IV Primary tabs Section 1. Full faith and credit shall be given in each state to the public acts, records, and judicial proceedings of every other state. And the Congress may by general laws prescribe the manner in which such acts, records, and proceedings shall be proved, and the effect thereof. Section 2. The citizens of each state shall be entitled to all privileges and immunities of citizens in the several states. A person charged in any state with treason, felony, or other crime, who shall flee from justice, and be found in another state, shall on demand of the executive authority of the state from which he fled, be delivered up, to be removed to the state having jurisdiction of the crime. No person held to service or labor in one state, under the laws thereof, escaping into another, shall, in consequence of any law or regulation therein, be discharged from such service or labor, but shall be delivered up on claim of the party to whom such service or labor may be due. Section 3. New states may be admitted by the Congress into this union; but no new states shall be formed or erected within the jurisdiction of any other state; nor any state be formed by the junction of two or more states, or parts of states, without the consent of the legislatures of the states concerned as well as of the Congress. The Congress shall have power to dispose of and make all needful rules and regulations respecting the territory or other property belonging to the United States; and nothing in this Constitution shall be so construed as to prejudice any claims of the United States, or of any particular state. Section 4. The United States shall guarantee to every state in this union a republican form of government, and shall protect each of them against invasion; and on application of the legislature, or of the executive (when the legislature cannot be convened) against domestic violence. ‹ Article III up Article V › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Ser
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from Delaware to Nevada despite the principal place of business for the parties being in Delaware and Colorado. The Law Explanation points to a potential violation of the Full Faith and Credit Clause if Nevada law conflicts with Delaware law, and Delaware has a significant interest in the case. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, "Full faith and credit shall be given in each state to the public acts, records, and judicial proceedings of every other state." By changing the governing law seemingly arbitrarily, the contract introduces a potential conflict that could undermine the Full Faith and Credit Clause, especially if Delaware law should apply due to the parties' location. This creates a legal contradiction because the choice of Nevada law could be viewed as an attempt to circumvent Delaware law, potentially making the contract unenforceable or leading to legal challenges.
